question: Tell me about yourself.

stories.title: Education & foundations
stories.situation: I’m Emmanuel Acheampong, finishing my B.S. in Computer Science at Grambling State University (May 2027). I’m on the Dean’s List (’24 and ’25), a Presidential Scholarship recipient, and I earned gold at the Vanda African Math Olympiad in 2018. Coursework that shaped me: Object-Oriented Programming, Software Engineering, Data Structures & Algorithms, and Operating Systems.
stories.task: What I want interviewers to remember: I don’t just take classes—I pair theory with internships and shipped products where impact is measurable.
stories.action: Alongside school I’ve interned at Geaux Network (Associate Software Developer, remote, summer 2025) and Hubtel LTD (Software Engineer Intern, Ghana, 2023). I’ve also built MedDiagnose AI (health-tech + ML), VisualizeMyAlgorithm (1,000+ learners), and a Mini-Compiler used by 50+ developers.
stories.result: The through-line is production engineering: tests, observability, and traceability—whether I’m on gRPC/protobuf services, a Kafka pipeline, or a React front end.

stories.title: Geaux Network — platform & traceability
stories.situation: At Geaux Network I worked with engineering and content on a Change History View in TypeScript and SCSS, pulling together multiple backend event sources so edits to podcasts and media metadata were auditable.
stories.task: Ship something useful for traceability without waiting for every legacy system to unify.
stories.action: I collaborated across teams to prioritize high-signal protobuf-backed events first, added integration coverage on the merged read path, and helped harden the Java/gRPC API layer—where we roughly doubled measured data accuracy on the ingestion path and supported podcast distribution to 10,000+ monthly listeners.
stories.result: Creators and admins got a clearer audit trail; releases stayed on schedule with a defined backlog for the remaining sources.

stories.title: Hubtel — real-time systems
stories.situation: At Hubtel LTD I built a scalable real-time pipeline with Apache Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL to process high-volume events for transaction-driven services, and I shipped 20+ automated tests (Spring Boot, JUnit, Postman) that materially improved reliability on payment workflows.
stories.task: Make failures visible faster without destabilizing money paths.
stories.action: I traced broker → sink, added metrics and tests at integration boundaries, and implemented a Firebase push-notification path for critical status updates—tuning delivery so critical pushes met sub-10ms latency targets where the stack allowed.
stories.result: Downtime detection improved by about 40% versus our baseline; reliability on targeted services rose roughly 80%; fewer repeat incidents on payment flows.

question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with your manager.

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: Geaux Network — Associate Software Developer Intern, May–Aug 2025, remote. Problem: engineering and content both needed audit trails for podcast and media edits, but events lived in multiple backends with inconsistent schemas.
stories.task: Goal: ship Change History (TypeScript, SCSS) that improves traceability on v1 without blocking the release on full consolidation.
stories.action: Steps: (1) Joint session with engineering + content to rank event sources by traceability value per engineering week. (2) Phase 1 UI: highest-signal protobuf-backed events first. (3) Integration tests on merged read path before adding sources.
stories.result: Outcome: stronger traceability for targeted workflows; stable release cadence; backlog defined for remaining sources.

stories.title: Spring Boot test expansion
stories.situation: Problem: failures appeared at integration boundaries (DB, messaging, HTTP), not in isolated unit tests — typical for payment-related Spring services.
stories.task: Goal: increase confidence on money-touching code paths.
stories.action: Steps: 20+ tests using Spring Boot, JUnit, Postman; cases centered on transaction boundaries, retries, and known failure modes.
stories.result: Outcome: ~80% reliability lift in targeted areas (team measurement); regressions caught pre-release more often.

stories.title: Cross-officer conflict
stories.situation: Conflict: officers split on hackathons vs corporate sessions vs K-12 outreach.
stories.task: Goal: single quarterly roadmap with measurable targets.
stories.action: Resolution: attendance data + survey; map sponsor dollars to dated events; vote written priority list in meeting.
stories.result: Outcome: fewer debate loops; execution tracked to agreed metrics.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: Event: ColorStack Winter Hackathon 2024 — ~1,000 participants worldwide.
stories.task: Goal: MVP + demo script with zero live-integration failure on stage.
stories.action: Execution: scope freeze day one; FE/BE owners; rehearsed demo; fallback if API unstable.
stories.result: Outcome: 1st place Winter 2024; demo completed without outage.

question: Tell me about a time you failed. How did you deal with the situation?

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Gap: engineers report accuracy; clinicians need override rules, failure modes, shift-time usability.
stories.task: Goal: assistive ML with explicit human checkpoints.
stories.action: Steps: plain-language model explanation in UI; documented uncertainty; UI blocks high-risk actions without clinician acknowledgment.
stories.result: Outcome: trust in rounds; automation limited to routing, alerts, eligibility prep — within hospital risk tolerance.

stories.title: Correctness first
stories.situation: Failure mode: lexer/parser edge cases produced corrupt IR; optimizations hid bugs.
stories.task: Goal: green parser/lexer suite before optimization passes.
stories.action: Steps: expand grammar tests; regression tokens; gate optimizations on passing baseline.
stories.result: Outcome: stable IR; other students added passes without mystery failures.

stories.title: gRPC service layer + tests
stories.situation: Context: internal services moving to Java + gRPC + Protocol Buffers for podcast ingestion and distribution (10,000+ monthly listeners). Ingestion errors propagate to creators and listeners.
stories.task: Goal: raise measured data accuracy on the ingestion path; keep protobuf contracts testable at the RPC boundary.
stories.action: Steps: extend Java service layer; tighten .proto files; add end-to-end tests across gRPC; validate against real ingestion runs, not mocks only.
stories.result: Outcome: ~2× measured accuracy on ingestion path (team metric); safer downstream integrations.

question: Describe a time when you led a team. What was the outcome?

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: Role: NSBE — General Secretary, Jan 2025–present, Grambling State University.
stories.task: Goal: grow technical membership; convert sponsor conversations into funded programs.
stories.action: Plan: officer alignment from member surveys; sponsor packets with attendance + outcomes; recurring technical events.
stories.result: Outcome: 300+ members; ~$3K technical partnerships (resume figure); higher CS engagement vs prior term.

stories.title: MedDiagnose stack + pilot
stories.situation: Project: MedDiagnose AI — Python, TypeScript, FastAPI, TensorFlow, React. Features: real-time monitoring, NIHSS scoring, RBAC, automated tPA eligibility.
stories.task: Goal: Louisiana hospital pilot optimized for workflow time — not leaderboard accuracy.
stories.action: Execution: FastAPI + React + model integration; Random Forest stroke-risk ~45% — positioned as decision support only; workflow KPIs agreed with neurologists.
stories.result: Outcome: pilot completed; ~35% reduction in time-to-treatment (pilot metric); assistive workflow, not autonomous diagnosis.

stories.title: VisualizeMyAlgorithm scale
stories.situation: Product: VisualizeMyAlgorithm (React) — pathfinding, sorting, NN-style demos. Reach: 1,000+ learners.
stories.task: Goal: classroom-scale performance + teaching clarity.
stories.action: Build: modular components (Dijkstra, A*, Minimax, NN demos); explicit step state machine; batched rendering pipeline.
stories.result: Outcome: ~40% faster self-reported time-to-understanding (informal classroom timing); stable under concurrent classroom load.

stories.result: Outcome: fewer wrong-role failures in QA; clearer audit trail; explicit creator/admin behavior.

question: Describe a time you received tough or critical feedback.

stories.title: Service-layer maintainability
stories.situation: Problem (Geaux): gRPC/protobuf code passed tests but was hard to extend — unclear module boundaries and error semantics for RPC callers.
stories.task: Goal: make protobuf packages and failures obvious to the next engineer.
stories.action: Steps: reorganize .proto packaging; add contract tests at RPC boundary; document error codes and retry expectations for consumers.
stories.result: Outcome: faster reviews; fewer integration surprises on the next features.

stories.title: ML calibration reality check
stories.situation: Problem: MedDiagnose dashboard led with raw ~45% RF accuracy — mentors flagged that non-technical readers would misinterpret it as diagnostic certainty.
stories.task: Goal: align reporting with how clinicians decide (workflow + calibration), not leaderboard metrics.
stories.action: Steps: add calibration / confusion context in UI copy; lead with time-to-treatment and pilot workflow KPIs; demote headline accuracy.
stories.result: Outcome: dashboard used in rounds; clinicians engaged because risk was framed correctly.

stories.title: Compiler scope creep
stories.situation: Problem: Mini-Compiler — I added optimization passes before lexer/parser tests covered edge tokens; reviewer flagged premature optimization.
stories.task: Goal: stabilize parse → IR before measuring optimization wins.
stories.action: Steps: freeze IR optimizations; expand lexer/parser regression suite; re-enable optimizations with before/after benchmarks.
stories.result: Outcome: stable compiler baseline; optimizations became credible and reproducible.

question: Describe a time when you had to give someone difficult feedback. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Peer tests at Hubtel
stories.situation: Problem: Teammate opened PR on Spring service; integration tests skipped a payment edge case I had seen fail in staging.
stories.task: Goal: block merge on evidence, not opinion — without personal attack.
stories.action: Steps: reproduce with Postman + attach logs; propose JUnit + Testcontainers case for that path; frame as shared quality bar.
stories.result: Outcome: tests added; PR merged; regression guarded.

stories.title: NSBE officer workload
stories.situation: Problem: Officer over-promised event attendance; sponsors and volunteers burned when numbers missed.
stories.task: Goal: reset expectations with data — private 1:1, respectful tone.
stories.action: Steps: show attendance vs promised; propose smaller committed events; separate critique of numbers from strengths (marketing).
stories.result: Outcome: next quarter hit sponsor-facing targets without volunteer burnout.

stories.title: Hackathon scope
stories.situation: Problem: ColorStack Winter — teammate wanted live ML API with <12h left; integration risk too high for demo.
stories.task: Goal: stable demo path + honest technical story.
stories.action: Steps: mock inference boundary; record golden outputs; rehearse failover.
stories.result: Outcome: on-time submission; winning demo with no live flake.

stories.task: That’s a strong start for where I am in my career.
stories.action: The growth edge is estimation and communication across teams—owning timelines without sandbagging or overpromising.
stories.result: Next step is bigger ownership with clearer SLOs and helping others on test design, not just my own code.

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: Role: NSBE — General Secretary, Jan 2025–present, Grambling State University.
stories.task: Goal: grow technical membership; convert sponsor conversations into funded programs.
stories.action: Plan: officer alignment from member surveys; sponsor packets with attendance + outcomes; recurring technical events.
stories.result: Outcome: 300+ members; ~$3K technical partnerships (resume figure); higher CS engagement vs prior term.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: Event: ColorStack Winter Hackathon 2024 — ~1,000 participants worldwide.
stories.task: Goal: MVP + demo script with zero live-integration failure on stage.
stories.action: Execution: scope freeze day one; FE/BE owners; rehearsed demo; fallback if API unstable.
stories.result: Outcome: 1st place Winter 2024; demo completed without outage.

question: What is your biggest strength and area of growth?

stories.title: Strength: end-to-end debugging
stories.situation: I’m pretty calm when things break across protobuf, Kafka and MQTT, and the UI—I’ve done that at Hubtel and Geaux.
stories.task: Where I’m growing is knowing when to pull someone in earlier.
stories.action: I can over-investigate before I escalate; I’m working on time-boxing and giving leads a tight summary with what I’ve ruled out.
stories.result: They get signal sooner, and I still finish the fix—I’m not dropping the ball.

stories.title: Strength: responsible ML framing
stories.situation: MedDiagnose taught me to talk about ML next to workflow outcomes, not a single accuracy number.
stories.task: I want more depth in real MLOps—not just training notebooks.
stories.action: I’m reading more about drift monitoring and what retraining looks like when you’re not in a classroom.
stories.result: I’m closing that gap with side projects and coursework where I can.

stories.title: Strength: community leadership
stories.situation: NSBE is proof I can execute—membership growth, real sponsor dollars.
stories.task: I’m still learning to delegate so it doesn’t all ride on me.
stories.action: I’m trying to build other leaders, not only run events myself.
stories.result: The win is when programs keep working even when I’m slammed with school.

question: What aspects of your work are most often criticized?

stories.title: Over-engineering risk
stories.situation: I’ve been called out for reaching for abstractions too early—I get excited about clean design.
stories.task: I’m learning to prove the need with data first.
stories.action: Now I try the smallest change that meets the SLO, and only refactor when the pattern repeats.
stories.result: PRs move faster and people spend less time debating architecture in the abstract.

stories.title: Estimation optimism
stories.situation: Compiler and viz projects taught me pretty visuals can hide a week of integration pain.
stories.task: I’m getting better at padding estimates for unknowns.
stories.action: I slice work vertically so there’s something demoable sooner.
stories.result: Stakeholders get fewer “surprise, we need another sprint” moments.

stories.title: Async updates
stories.situation: Remote work means nobody sees you thinking—mentors told me I need to over-communicate status.
stories.task: I’m working on smaller, more frequent updates.
stories.action: Short standup notes or doc updates when something shifts.
stories.result: Managers stopped pinging me for “where are we?” because it’s already written down.

question: Tell me about a time you needed information from someone who wasn't responsive.

stories.title: Blocked on proto review
stories.situation: A senior engineer had my protobuf review sitting, and my Geaux feature was stuck behind it.
stories.task: I couldn’t spam them, but I couldn’t wait forever either.
stories.action: I sent a tight summary of the diff, listed exact questions, and offered two short slots. If it slipped past what we’d agreed, I escalated with facts—who was blocked downstream.
stories.result: The review landed, and they actually thanked me for making it easy to consume.

stories.title: Clinical feedback loop
stories.situation: During finals, neurologists weren’t answering MedDiagnose UX questions fast—and honestly, their time is more valuable than mine.
stories.task: I needed answers without being annoying.
stories.action: I batched questions, sent screenshots, and used short Loom videos so they could respond async.
stories.result: We got what we needed without burning bridges.

stories.title: Sponsor contact ghosting
stories.situation: For NSBE, a sponsor contact went quiet right when we needed a yes on budget so we could book travel.
stories.task: I needed an answer without sounding desperate.
stories.action: I sent a short email with two clear options—commit by this date or pause—and only looped a faculty advisor when it was fair.
stories.result: We got a decision in time to book or pivot to another sponsor without drama.

question: What frustrates you?

stories.title: Opaque incidents
stories.situation: Nothing frustrates me more than a postmortem that says “fixed” with no root cause and no follow-up tests.
stories.task: If we don’t learn, we’ll see the same fire again.
stories.action: At Hubtel I pushed for a real timeline, what actually failed, and what test would catch it next time—not just “we restarted it.”
stories.result: Once we fixed upstream backpressure instead of blaming Postgres, repeat noise dropped.

stories.title: Vanity metrics
stories.situation: Shipping features with no definition of success drives me nuts—if we can’t point to latency, errors, or a user outcome, what are we doing?
stories.task: I want work tied to something measurable.
stories.action: On MedDiagnose I’d rather talk about time-to-treatment than a pretty slide.
stories.result: Teams move faster when everyone agrees what “good” means.

stories.title: Gatekeeping
stories.situation: I get frustrated by communities that make beginners feel dumb—that’s a big reason I care about NSBE and ColorStack.
stories.task: Gatekeeping wastes talent.
stories.action: I help run intro workshops—DSA basics, git hygiene—so people have a real on-ramp.
stories.result: Retention goes up when people feel invited, not tested for coolness.

question: What is something 90% of people disagree with you about?

stories.title: Move slow to move fast
stories.situation: A lot of people want to hotfix on Friday and call it heroism. I’d rather ship a boring, tested change Monday.
stories.task: Tail risk matters more than adrenaline.
stories.action: Hubtel and MedDiagnose both taught me untested hotfixes come back as outages.
stories.result: I’d rather be the person who sleeps and keeps prod stable.

stories.title: Accuracy is not enough in ML
stories.situation: Hackathons love leaderboard scores. I care whether the model behaves in the real workflow—calibration, when it’s wrong, how clinicians actually use it.
stories.task: That’s not always a popular take in a 48-hour room.
stories.action: I push for human-in-the-loop UX and monitoring, not a vanity metric.
stories.result: Clinicians buy in when you’re honest; some hackathon judges don’t love it until they see pilot numbers.

stories.title: Community > grind culture
stories.situation: Some people brag about all-nighters. I don’t think that’s sustainable—or necessary.
stories.task: I’d rather ship consistently and stay healthy.
stories.action: I time-box, protect sleep during exams, and still shipped real projects at Geaux and on VisualizeMyAlgorithm.
stories.result: Dean’s List plus shipping beats burnout theater.

question: Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult or bad news to someone.

stories.title: Model limits to stakeholders
stories.situation: I had to tell clinical partners the stroke risk model was sitting around 45% accuracy—that’s not what someone wants to hear if they wanted a headline “AI saves lives” story.
stories.task: Bad news lands better if you’re direct.
stories.action: I paired it with what we could prove on the workflow side—NIHSS, tPA checks, and about 35% faster time-to-treatment in the pilot.
stories.result: They could live with it as decision support, and the pilot kept going with clearer expectations.

stories.title: Sponsor shortfall
stories.situation: NSBE had to tell a sponsor we didn’t hit the attendance we promised for an event—that’s uncomfortable.
stories.task: We owned it instead of making excuses.
stories.action: We came back with a smaller workshop, guaranteed RSVPs, and a tighter plan.
stories.result: They stayed with us because we didn’t hide the miss.

stories.title: Demo failure prep
stories.situation: Six hours before a ColorStack deadline I told my team the live ML endpoint was too risky—we’d been fighting instability.
stories.task: I’d rather eat crow early than fail on stage.
stories.action: We stubbed inference with recorded outputs, kept the API contract stable, and rehearsed.
stories.result: We still won Winter 2024 out of roughly a thousand people—and the demo didn’t flake.
